content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 10, 2016

Jolie Houston
City Attorney, City of Los Altos
Berliner Cohen LLP
Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor
San Jose, CA 95113-2233

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-075

Dear Ms. Houston:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Los Altos City Councilmember Janis Pepper regarding Section 1090.¹ Because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented. Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090 and not under the Political Reform Act, common law conflict of interest, or common law rule against incompatible offices.

When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section 1090, the Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) Accordingly, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).)

Finally, the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from continuing to participate in the interview process for the position of Contract Interim Chief Executive Officer ("Contract CEO") with the Silicon Valley Clean Energy Authority (the "SVCEA"), from being hired for that position, or from later applying for the position of full-time Chief Executive Officer ("full-time CEO") with the SVCEA, interviewing for that position, and being hired for that position?

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No. Based on the facts presented, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from
further pursuing or being hired for the Contract CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later
pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos and the authorized representative of City
Councilmember Janis Pepper. The Councilmember has worked in the energy and electric utility
industry for the last 30 years and currently has a full-time position as Electric Division Manager
with Silicon Valley Power, the municipal electric utility serving the City of Santa Clara.

Community Choice Aggregation

Authorized pursuant to Public Utilities Code Section 366.2, Community Choice
Aggregation ("CCA") enables city and county governments to pool the electricity demand within
their jurisdictions to directly procure or generate electrical power supplies on behalf of the residents
and businesses in their communities. While electricity supply is purchased by the CCA program,
the incumbent utility remains responsible for maintaining the electricity grid and for providing
customer service. The City of Los Altos is within the County of Santa Clara, and the incumbent
utility within the County is PG&E.

City of Los Altos Joins the SVCEA

Public Utilities Code Section 366.2(c)(12)(B) authorizes a joint powers authority made up of
cities and counties to conduct a CCA program. During 2015, the County and 11 cities in the
County, including the City of Los Altos, participated in a study of whether a CCA program was
technically feasible and whether a joint powers authority should be formed to conduct such a
program. In the latter part of 2015, a draft joint powers agreement for the formation of the SVCEA
was presented to the interested local government agencies for adoption.

Public Utilities Code Section 366.2(c)(12)(A) requires an entity authorized to be a
community choice aggregator that elects to implement a CCA program within its jurisdiction to do
so by ordinance, and authorizes a city, county, or city and county to request another entity
authorized to be a community choice aggregator to act as the community choice aggregator within
its jurisdiction by resolution.

On February 9, 2016, the Councilmember participated in the Los Altos City Council vote on
whether to adopt Los Altos Ordinance No. 2016-419, authorizing the implementation of a CCA
program in the City. At the same meeting, the Councilmember also participated in the vote on
whether to adopt Resolution No. 2016-06, approving the SVCEA Joint Powers Agreement
("SVCEA JPA") and authorizing the City to become a member of the SVCEA. The City Council
unanimously approved both the ordinance and the resolution.

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 3

Terms of the SVCEA JPA

The SVCEA Board of Directors is made up of 12 members, eleven from member cities and towns, including the City of Los Altos, and one from the County. Each member local government agency has one representative on the SVCEA Board, and may remove its representative at the pleasure of the agency's governing body.

The SVCEA is a Joint Powers Authority established pursuant to state law and a separate legal entity from its member agencies. State law and the SVCEA JPA provide that the debts, liabilities, and obligations of the SVCEA are not the debts, liabilities, or obligations of its member agencies. The SVCEA Board has the exclusive right to amend the SVCEA JPA.

The SVCEA JPA does not authorize member agencies to ratify or veto decisions of the SVCEA Board. You state that the member agencies of the SVCEA do not have any supervisorial control over the actions of the SVCEA Board. However, each member agency may replace its representative to the SVCEA Board at the pleasure of the member agency's governing body, and a member agency may withdraw from the SVCEA subject to notice and financial obligation requirements imposed by the SVCEA JPA.

The Contract CEO and Full-Time CEO Positions

Although Section 2.5.2 of the SVCEA JPA authorizes the SVCEA Board of Directors to appoint a Chief Executive Officer, the SVCEA JPA does not require this position to be created. The Board of Directors have several options on how to administer the SVCEA, including hiring a contract or employee administrator, hiring one of the member agencies to administer the SVCEA, or hiring an outside firm or entity to perform this function.

The Cities of Cupertino and Sunnyvale have contracted with the SVCEA to administer its inception during the interim period before the SVCEA Board is sworn in, and on February 10, 2016, the City of Cupertino issued a Request for Qualifications ("RFQ") for a Contract CEO position.

On March 7, 2016, the Councilmember submitted a cover letter and resume in response to the RFQ for the Contract CEO position. On March 16, 2016, the Councilmember received an email from the City of Cupertino requesting that she participate in an interview for the position. On March 24, 2016, the Councilmember interviewed for the position along with a number of other candidates. The interview panel did not consist of any prospective SVCEA Board members, and the Councilmember has not had any conversations with prospective SVCEA Board members from other agencies regarding her application to become the Contract CEO.

At the first meeting of the SVCEA Board, the Board will decide whether to appoint an ad hoc committee of Board members to interview candidates for the Contract CEO position. In approximately 6 months, or as directed by the SVCEA Board, it is expected that the SVCEA will be seeking a full-time CEO. The final decisions on whether to hire a Contract CEO or a full-time CEO will be made by the SVCEA Board. The SVCEA Board will select its Contract CEO and full-time CEO without any review by the governing bodies of the SVCEA's members.

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 4

At this time, the Councilmember is a candidate for the Contract CEO position. She will be
on a list of eligible candidates presented to the ad hoc committee if this committee is appointed by
the SVCEA Board. The City of Los Altos representative on the SVCEA Board will not participate
in the ad hoc committee that will make a hiring recommendation for the Contract CEO position to
the SVCEA Board.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides in pertinent part as follows:

"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city
officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made
by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are
members."

Section 1090 is concerned with any financial interest, other than a remote or minimal
interest, that would prevent a public official from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided
allegiance to his or her public entity. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The
purpose of Section 1090 is to "remove or limit the possibility of any personal influence, either
directly or indirectly, which might bear on an official's decision." (Ibid.) Thus, Section 1090 is
"aimed at eliminating temptation, avoiding the appearance of impropriety, and assuring the
government of the officer's undivided and uncompromised allegiance." (People v. Honig (1996) 48
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.)

A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)
The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all
parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) When an officer with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the
governing body of a public entity, the prohibition of Section 1090 extends to the entire body. (89
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006).)

We employ the following analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of interest
under Section 1090.

Is the Councilmember subject to the provisions of Section 1090?

Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member
bodies, whether elected or appointed. City officers such as city councilmembers are subject to
Section 1090. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at p. 645.) Therefore, the Councilmember is subject to
Section 1090.

Does the decision at issue involve a contract?

Public employment constitutes a "contract" for purposes of Section 1090. (Finnegan v.
Schrader (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 572, 580-581; 81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 274, 278 (1998).)

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 5

The Councilmember is pursuing, and is in the running for, the Contract CEO position with
the SVCEA. In the near future, the Councilmember may also pursue, and the SVCEA may offer
her, the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA. Thus, the SVCEA's offer of public employment,
with respect to either the Contract CEO position or the full-time CEO position, if accepted by the
Councilmember, would constitute a contract for purposes of Section 1090.

Is the Councilmember making or participating in making a contract?

Becoming a Member Agency of the SVCEA

We first consider whether the Councilmember made or participated in the making of the
potential employment contracts between the SVCEA and its prospective Contract CEO or full-time
CEO as a result of the City of Los Altos becoming a member agency of the SVCEA.

For purposes of Section 1090, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as
any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing
of plans and specifications, and solicitations for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v.
City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237; see also Stigall v. City of Taft, supra, at p. 569.)
To substantiate a violation of Section 1090, however, it must be established that the public official
"had the opportunity to and did influence" the contract at issue either directly or indirectly to
promote his or her personal interests. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052; 81
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 317, 320 (1998); 66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 156, 160 (1983).)

The Los Altos City Council participated in a study regarding the feasibility and
implementation of CCA program, adopted an ordinance to implement the program, and adopted a
resolution approving the draft SVCEA JPA and authorizing the City to be a member of the SVCEA.
Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that the Councilmember had the opportunity to
and did influence the potential public employment contracts between the SVCEA and its Contract
CEO or its full-time CEO.

Furthermore, while Section 2.5.2 of the SVCEA JPA authorizes the SVCEA Board of
Directors to appoint a Chief Executive Officer, it does not require this position to be created. The
SVCEA Board has several options on how to administer the SVCEA, including hiring a contract or
employee administrator, hiring one of the member agencies to administer the SVCEA, or hiring an
outside firm or entity to perform this function. This provision of the SVCEA JPA indicates that the
Councilmember did not influence the making of any potential public employment contract between
the SVCEA and the Contract CEO or the full-time CEO because the SVCEA Board has the
authority to determine how the SVCEA is administrated and could implement an administration
scheme that does not utilize a Contract CEO or a full-time CEO.

Thus, we find that the Councilmember did not have the opportunity to and did not influence
the potential public employment contracts between the SVCEA and its prospective Contract CEO
or full-time CEO.

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 6

Being a Member of the Governing Body of a Member Agency of the SVCEA

We now turn to the question of whether the Councilmember makes or participates in the
making of the potential employment contracts between the SVCEA and its prospective Contract
CEO or full-time CEO by being a member of the Los Altos City Council, the governing body of a
member agency of the SVCEA.

The Attorney General has opined that a county board of supervisors does not make or
participate in the making of its county housing authority's public employment contract with a
prospective secretary and executive director of the authority because the authority is a separate
body and because the board of supervisors has no role to play in the authority's hiring decisions.
(81 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 274, 277-278 (1998).) At issue in that opinion was whether Section 1090
precluded a county supervisor from being named the secretary and executive director of the
county's housing authority. (Id. at p. 277.)

"Significantly, it is the housing authority commissioners who would hire
the supervisor as their employee. The commissioners act as an administrative
arm of the state in pursuing the state concern of developing new housing
opportunities. (Housing Authority v. City of L.A. (1982) 38 Cal.2d 853, 862.)
The board of supervisors is a separate body and has no role to play in the
commissioners' hiring decisions. (§ 34278.) Hence, it would be the housing
authority, not the board of supervisors, that would 'make' the employment
contract with the supervisor for purposes of Government Code section 1090."

The Attorney General noted that although the housing authority commissioners were subject to
removal for cause by the board of supervisors, the commission's independent contracting powers
would not be affected by such limited supervisorial control for purposes of Section 1090. (Id. at p.
279.) In other words, this alone would not constitute making or participating in the making of the
potential employment contract. Accordingly, the Attorney General concluded that Section 1090 did
not prohibit the county supervisor from being hired as the authority's secretary and executive
director. (Ibid.)

The SVCEA is a Joint Powers Authority established pursuant to state law and a separate
legal entity from its member agencies. State law and the SVCEA JPA provide that the debts,
liabilities, and obligations of the SVCEA are not the debts, liabilities, or obligations of its member
agencies.

The SVCEA JPA does not authorize its member agencies to ratify or veto the specific
employment contract in question. The final decisions on whether to hire a Contract CEO or a full-
time CEO will be made by the SVCEA Board, and the SVCEA Board will select its Contract CEO
and full-time CEO without any review by the governing bodies of the SVCEA's members. The Los
Altos representative on the SVCEA Board will not participate in the ad hoc interview committee
that will make a recommendation to the SVCEA Board, and the Councilmember has not had any
conversations with prospective SVCEA Board members from other agencies regarding her
application to become the Contract CEO.

File No. A-16-075
Page No. 7

With respect to the Los Altos City Council's control over the SVCEA, each member agency, including the City, may replace its representative to the SVCEA Board at the pleasure of the member agency's governing body, and a member agency may withdraw from the SVCEA subject to notice and financial obligation requirements imposed by the SVCEA JPA. The facts presented do not indicate that a member agency has any other control over the SVCEA decision in question. Therefore, the Los Altos City Council's involvement in the decision, similar to that of the board of supervisors' involvement in the decision at issue in the Attorney General's opinion, would not constitute making or participating in the making of that decision for purposes of Section 1090.

Thus, neither the Los Altos City Council nor the Councilmember is making or participating in the making of the SVCEA's potential public employment contracts with a Contract CEO or a full-time CEO, and our Section 1090 analysis ends here. Accordingly, we conclude that Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from further pursuing or being hired for the Contract CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does Section I 090 prohibit the Councilmember from continuing to participate in the 
interview process for the position of Contract Interim Chief Executive Officer ("Contract CEO") 
with the Silicon Valley Clean Energy Authority (the .. SVCEA"), from being hired for that position, 
or from later applying for the position of full-time Chief Executive Officer ("full-time CEO") with 
the SVCEA, interviewing for that position, and being hired for that position? 
1
sections.conclusion:
No. Based on the facts presented, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from 
further pursuing or being hired for the Contract CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later 
pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 provides in pertinent part as follows: 
"Members of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city 
officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made 
by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are

interest, that would prevent a public official from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided

"aimed at eliminating temptation, avoiding the appearance of impropriety, and assuring the 
government of the officer's undivided and uncompromised allegiance." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) 
A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The prohibition applies regardless of whether the tenns of the contract are fair and equitable to all 
parties. (Id. at pp. 646-649.) When an officer with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the 
governing body of a public entity, the prohibition of Section I 090 extends to the entire body. (89 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49, 50 (2006).) 
We employ the following analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of interest 
under Section I 090. 
Is the Councilmember subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. City officers such as city councilmembers are subject to 
Section l 090. (Thomson v. Call, supra, at p. 645.) Therefore, the Councilmember is subject to 
Section I 090. 
Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
Public employment constitutes a "contract" for purposes of Section 1090. (Finnegan v.

the SVCEA. In the near future, the Councilmember may also pursue, and the SVCEA may offer

Is the Councilmeber making or participating in making a contract?

To substantiate a violation of Section l 090, however, it must be established that the public official

The Los Altos City Council participated in a study regarding the feasibility and 
implementation of CCA program, adopted an ordinance to implement the program, and adopted a

Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that the Councilmember had the opportunity to

subject to notice and financial obligation requirements imposed by the SVCEA JPA. The facts 
presented do not indicate that a member agency has any other control over the SVCEA decision in 
question. Therefore, the Los Altos City Council's involvement in the decision, similar to that of the 
board of supervisors' involvement in the decision at issue in the Attorney General's opinion, would 
not constitute making or participating in the making of that decision for purposes of Section 1090. 
Thus, neither the Los Altos City Council nor the Councilmember is making or participating 
in the making of the SVCEA's potential public employment contracts with a Contract CEO or a 
full-time CEO, and our Section 1090 analysis ends here. Accordingly, we conclude that Section 
l 090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from further pursuing or being hired for the Contract 
CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position 
with the SVCEA. 
Cf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl

QUESTION: Does Section I 090 prohibit the Councilmember from continuing to participate in the 
interview process for the position of Contract Interim Chief Executive Officer ("Contract CEO") 
with the Silicon Valley Clean Energy Authority (the .. SVCEA"), from being hired for that position, 
or from later applying for the position of full-time Chief Executive Officer ("full-time CEO") with 
the SVCEA, interviewing for that position, and being hired for that position? 
1

CONCLUSION: No. Based on the facts presented, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from 
further pursuing or being hired for the Contract CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later 
pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 10, 2016 Jolie Houston City Attorney, City of Los Altos Berliner Cohen LLP Ten Almaden Blvd., 11th Floor San Jose, CA 95113-2233 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-075 Dear Ms. Houston: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Los Altos City Councilmember Janis Pepper regarding Section 1090.¹ Because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented. Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090 and not under the Political Reform Act, common law conflict of interest, or common law rule against incompatible offices. When a request for advice involves potential issues raised under Section 1090, the Commission is required to forward a copy of the request to the Attorney General's Office and the local district attorney prior to proceeding with the advice. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) Accordingly, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Finally, the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit the Councilmember from continuing to participate in the interview process for the position of Contract Interim Chief Executive Officer ("Contract CEO") with the Silicon Valley Clean Energy Authority (the "SVCEA"), from being hired for that position, or from later applying for the position of full-time Chief Executive Officer ("full-time CEO") with the SVCEA, interviewing for that position, and being hired for that position? ¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-075 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION No. Based on the facts presented, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Councilmember from further pursuing or being hired for the Contract CEO position with the SVCEA, or from later pursuing or being hired for the full-time CEO position with the SVCEA. FACTS You are the City Attorney for the City of Los Altos and the authorized representative of City Councilmember Janis Pepper. The Councilmember has worked in the energy and electric utility industry for the last 30 years and currently has a full-time position as Electric Division Manager with Silicon Valley Power, the municipal electric utility serving the City of Santa Clara. Community Choice Aggregation Authorized pursuant to Public Utilities Code Section 366.2, Community Choice Aggregation ("CCA") enables city and county governments to pool the electricity demand within their jurisdictions to directly procure or generate electrical power supplies on behalf of the residents and businesses in their communities. While electricity supply is purchased by the CCA program, the incumbent utility remains responsible for maintaining the electricity grid and for providing customer service. The City of
